tree.setup: You've stumbled into one of Beijing's last remaining traditional public bathhouses. The owner, Boss Ma, is a stocky man in his sixties who runs the place almost alone now. It's late afternoon — the quiet hours between the morning elderly crowd and the occasional evening visitor. Steam drifts through the tiled room as he wipes down benches.
tree.setup_zh: 你无意中走进了北京最后几家传统公共澡堂之一。老板马老板是一位六十多岁的壮实男人，现在几乎独自经营着这个地方。现在是下午——在早上老年人群和偶尔的晚间客人之间的安静时段。蒸汽在贴瓷砖的房间里飘荡，他正在擦拭长凳。

turns.speaker: npc
turns.text_zh: 年轻人难得来这种地方了。现在大家都在家洗，或者去那种日式汤泉——灯光暗暗的，放着轻音乐，一个人泡一个池子。我这里不一样，我这里是大通铺，谁都看得见谁。以前的人不觉得这有什么，现在的人受不了。
turns.text_pinyin: Niánqīngrén nándé lái zhè zhǒng dìfang le. Xiànzài dàjiā dōu zài jiā xǐ, huòzhě qù nà zhǒng Rìshì tāngquán — dēngguāng àn àn de, fàng zhe qīng yīnyuè, yīge rén pào yīge chízi. Wǒ zhèlǐ bù yīyàng, wǒ zhèlǐ shì dà tōngpù, shéi dōu kàn de jiàn shéi. Yǐqián de rén bù juéde zhè yǒu shénme, xiànzài de rén shòu bùliǎo.
turns.text_en: It's rare for young people to come to a place like this anymore. Now everyone showers at home, or goes to those Japanese-style hot spring spas — dim lighting, soft music, one person per pool. My place is different — open layout, everyone can see everyone. People in the past thought nothing of it; people now can't handle it.

turns.speaker: player
turns.prompt_en: What do you say to him?

options.text_zh: 在大通铺里，大家都一样，没有衣服来标识身份。这种平等感，私密的地方给不了。
options.text_pinyin: Zài dà tōngpù lǐ, dàjiā dōu yīyàng, méiyǒu yīfu lái biāozhì shēnfèn. Zhè zhǒng píngděng gǎn, sīmì de dìfang gěi bùliǎo.
options.text_en: In an open bathhouse, everyone is the same — no clothes to signal status. That kind of equality is something private spaces can't give you.
options.feedback: 马老板停下擦台布，点点头：「你说到点上了。就是这个。」

options.text_zh: 日式汤泉挺好的啊，环境比这里好多了。
options.text_pinyin: Rìshì tāngquán tǐng hǎo de a, huánjìng bǐ zhèlǐ hǎo duō le.
options.text_en: Japanese-style hot springs are great though — the atmosphere is much nicer.
options.feedback: 马老板笑了笑，没有反驳，继续擦台。

options.text_zh: 现在大家都很注意个人隐私，所以才不来这种地方。
options.text_pinyin: Xiànzài dàjiā dōu hěn zhùyì gèrén yǐnsī, suǒyǐ cái bù lái zhè zhǒng dìfang.
options.text_en: Everyone cares so much about personal privacy these days — that's probably why they don't come.
options.feedback: 马老板耸了耸肩：「是，不来了。」他没有接着说什么。

turns.speaker: npc
turns.text_zh: 你说到点上了——平等。我记得小时候，我爸带我来澡堂，隔壁泡着的可能是局长，也可能是蹬三轮的。在热水里，谁也不比谁高。我爸说，澡堂是最诚实的地方，因为你什么都藏不住。
turns.text_pinyin: Nǐ shuō dào diǎn shàng le — píngděng. Wǒ jìde xiǎo shíhòu, wǒ bà dài wǒ lái zǎotáng, gébì pào zhe de kěnéng shì júzhǎng, yě kěnéng shì dēng sānlún de. Zài rè shuǐ lǐ, shéi yě bù bǐ shéi gāo. Wǒ bà shuō, zǎotáng shì zuì chéngshí de dìfang, yīnwèi nǐ shénme dōu cáng bú zhù.
turns.text_en: You've hit it exactly — equality. I remember when I was a kid, my dad would bring me to the bathhouse. The person soaking next to us might be a bureau chief or a rickshaw driver. In hot water, nobody's above anyone. My dad used to say, the bathhouse is the most honest place, because you can't hide anything.

turns.speaker: player
turns.prompt_en: What do you say to him?

options.text_zh: 「什么都藏不住」——字面上是身体，但我觉得那种氛围里，人也更容易说真话。放松了，防御就下来了。
options.text_pinyin: 'Shénme dōu cáng bú zhù' — zìmiàn shàng shì shēntǐ, dàn wǒ juéde nà zhǒng fēnwéi lǐ, rén yě gèng róngyì shuō zhēnhuà. Fàngsōng le, fángyù jiù xià lái le.
options.text_en: 'Can't hide anything' — literally the body, but I think in that atmosphere people also find it easier to say what's true. Once you relax, the defenses come down.
options.feedback: 马老板把抹布搭在肩上，认真点头：「你说得对。这里谈出来的事，比办公室多得多。」

options.text_zh: 您爸爸还健在吗？他也是开澡堂的吗？
options.text_pinyin: Nín bàba hái jiànzài ma? Tā yě shì kāi zǎotáng de ma?
options.text_en: Is your father still alive? Did he also run a bathhouse?
options.feedback: 马老板停了一下：「走了。」他没有继续说下去。

options.text_zh: 局长和蹬三轮的坐在一起——那时候社会差距没那么大？
options.text_pinyin: Júzhǎng hé dēng sānlún de zuò zài yīqǐ — nà shíhòu shèhuì chājù méi nàme dà?
options.text_en: A bureau chief and a rickshaw driver in the same pool — was the gap between people smaller back then?
options.feedback: 马老板想了想：「差距一直都有。就是这里不分。」他继续擦长凳。

turns.speaker: npc
turns.text_zh: 对，很多事在澡堂里比在办公室里好谈。因为热水让人放松，放松了防御就下来了。我这些年见过太多了——有人在澡堂里和好了，有人在澡堂里哭了，还有人每天来不是为了洗澡，就是为了有人说话。特别是那些独居的老人。
turns.text_pinyin: Duì, hěn duō shì zài zǎotáng lǐ bǐ zài bàngōngshì lǐ hǎo tán. Yīnwèi rè shuǐ ràng rén fàngsōng, fàngsōng le fángyù jiù xià lái le. Wǒ zhèxiē nián jiàn guò tài duō le — yǒu rén zài zǎotáng lǐ hé hǎo le, yǒu rén zài zǎotáng lǐ kū le, hái yǒu rén měi tiān lái bú shì wèile xǐzǎo, jiù shì wèile yǒu rén shuōhuà. Tèbié shì nàxiē dújū de lǎorén.
turns.text_en: Right, many things are easier to discuss in a bathhouse than in an office. Because hot water relaxes people, and once relaxed, defenses come down. Over the years I've seen too much — people reconciling in the bathhouse, people crying in the bathhouse, and people who come every day not to bathe, but just to have someone to talk to. Especially the elderly who live alone.

turns.speaker: player
turns.prompt_en: What do you say to him?

options.text_zh: 对那些老人来说，每天来泡个澡、跟人聊几句，可能是一整天唯一的社交。如果这里关了，那个空缺很难补上。
options.text_pinyin: Duì nàxiē lǎorén lái shuō, měi tiān lái pào ge zǎo, gēn rén liáo jǐ jù, kěnéng shì yī zhěng tiān wéiyī de shèjiāo. Rúguǒ zhèlǐ guān le, nàge kòngquē hěn nán bǔ shàng.
options.text_en: For those elderly people, a daily soak and a few words with someone might be their only social contact all day. If this place closes, that gap would be hard to fill.
options.feedback: 马老板把抹布放下，叹了口气：「这我知道。所以我一直没舍得关。」

options.text_zh: 老人们可以去社区活动中心啊，那里也有人陪。
options.text_pinyin: Lǎorén men kěyǐ qù shèqū huódòng zhōngxīn a, nàlǐ yě yǒu rén péi.
options.text_en: The elderly can go to community activity centers — there are people there to spend time with.
options.feedback: 马老板摇摇头：「活动中心要报名，要参加活动。这里不用。进来坐着就行。」

options.text_zh: 您这里的卫生条件怎么样？定期消毒吗？
options.text_pinyin: Nín zhèlǐ de wèishēng tiáojiàn zěnmeyàng? Dìngqī xiāodú ma?
options.text_en: What are the hygiene conditions like here? Do you disinfect regularly?
options.feedback: 马老板直起腰，认真地说：「每天。卫生局每年来查，从来没出过事。」

turns.speaker: npc
turns.text_zh: 无声的隔离——你说得真准。有个老李头，七十多岁了，老婆走了五年了，儿子在深圳。他每天下午两点准时来，泡到四点。有一次我问他为什么每天都来，他说「因为水是热的，人是活的」。就这两个条件——热水和活人——在家里他一个都没有。
turns.text_pinyin: Wúshēng de gélí — nǐ shuō de zhēn zhǔn. Yǒu ge Lǎo Lǐ tóu, qīshí duō suì le, lǎopó zǒu le wǔ nián le, érzi zài Shēnzhèn. Tā měi tiān xiàwǔ liǎng diǎn zhǔnshí lái, pào dào sì diǎn. Yǒu yī cì wǒ wèn tā wèishéme měi tiān dōu lái, tā shuō 'yīnwèi shuǐ shì rè de, rén shì huó de'. Jiù zhè liǎng ge tiáojiàn — rè shuǐ hé huó rén — zài jiā lǐ tā yīge dōu méiyǒu.
turns.text_en: Silent isolation — that's exactly right. There's an Old Li, over seventy, wife passed five years ago, son's in Shenzhen. He comes at exactly two every afternoon, soaks until four. Once I asked him why he comes every day. He said, 'Because the water is hot and the people are alive.' Just those two conditions — hot water and living people — at home he has neither.

turns.speaker: player
turns.prompt_en: What do you say to him?

options.text_zh: 「水是热的，人是活的」——就这两件事。这可能是我听过的对孤独最准确的描述。马老板，对他来说，您这里不是消费的地方，是他每天的理由。
options.text_pinyin: 'Shuǐ shì rè de, rén shì huó de' — jiù zhè liǎng jiàn shì. Zhè kěnéng shì wǒ tīng guò de duì gūdú zuì zhǔnquè de miáoshù. Mǎ lǎobǎn, duì tā lái shuō, nín zhèlǐ bú shì xiāofèi de dìfang, shì tā měi tiān de lǐyóu.
options.text_en: 'The water is hot and the people are alive' — that might be the most accurate description of loneliness I've heard. Boss Ma, for him this isn't somewhere to spend money — it's his reason to get up each day.
options.feedback: 马老板沉默了一会儿，轻声说：「所以我不关。」

options.text_zh: 他儿子应该多回来看看他，不能光在外面挣钱。
options.text_pinyin: Tā érzi yīnggāi duō huílái kàn kan tā, bù néng guāng zài wàimiàn zhèngqián.
options.text_en: His son should visit more — you can't just be out there making money.
options.feedback: 马老板点点头：「是。但儿子也不容易。」话题就此打住。

options.text_zh: 他可以养只猫或者狗，有个伴就好了。
options.text_pinyin: Tā kěyǐ yǎng zhī māo huòzhě gǒu, yǒu ge bàn jiù hǎo le.
options.text_en: He could get a cat or dog — having a companion would help.
options.feedback: 马老板想了想：「他说过，老了养不动了。」

tree.cultural_note: Traditional public bathhouses (澡堂 zǎotáng) were once essential social infrastructure in Chinese cities, serving as spaces of hygiene, relaxation, and community before most homes had private bathing facilities. Their decline reflects both improved housing and a cultural shift toward privacy and individualism. For elderly residents — particularly men who grew up with communal bathing culture — the bathhouse remains one of the last remaining 'third places' where social bonds are maintained without the formality of organized activities.
